CASE FILE NO. JN-4B / 2026 — OPEN 27 JUN 2026 · JAYANAGAR 4TH BLOCK, BLR

Built for
some. Not for all.

A field audit of Jayanagar 4th Block, Bengaluru — one of the city's most developed neighbourhoods, and one where a staircase, a step, or an unmarked crossing still decides who gets to use that development.

4.0
Investment-linked score
2.0
Maintenance-linked score
10
Sites documented

Thesis

Growth is visible. Development is uneven.

By conventional economic measures — property values near the top of Bengaluru's market, dense commerce, real transit investment — Jayanagar 4th Block reads as an unambiguous development success. Amartya Sen's Capability Approach asks a harder question: has that growth become a real freedom to move, work, and seek healthcare for every resident, not only the able-bodied majority?

What growth statistics show

Rising land value, retail turnover, a functioning bus terminal, tree-lined avenues, a well-kept park. Every headline indicator points up.

What a wheelchair shows

A staircase-only shopping complex, a medical store with no ramp, an unmarked crossing outside the busiest transit hub — the same neighbourhood, measured by who can actually use it.

Field Evidence

Ten sites, geo-tagged and time-stamped

Deliberately curated to show contrast rather than a random sample — the same locality, often the same afternoon, with and without the design and maintenance that determine who can use it.

▸ The developed face of Jayanagar

✓ ACCESS

Public Amenities

Neighbourhood Park

35th Cross Rd, 4th Block

A flat, continuous, shaded walking track with outdoor gym equipment. Structurally, this is exactly the baseline the rest of the audit finds missing elsewhere — proof the standard exists, just not everywhere.

Streetscape

Palm-lined Avenue

36th Cross Rd, 4th Block

A wide, palm-lined avenue with a generous paved verge separating people from traffic. Photographed a kilometre and eight minutes from a garbage-blocked footpath on the very same street.

12.92348, 77.58321  ·  27 Jun 2026, 02:11 PM

✓ ACCESS

Mobility & Transport

TTMC Bus Terminal

4th T Block East

An organised, sheltered bus terminal with clear bay numbering. Genuinely good transit infrastructure — though still without a tactile guide strip or audible announcements.

12.92860, 77.58463  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:26 PM

▸ Development not accessible to all

✕ BARRIER

Accessibility · Core Evidence

Staircase-Only Access

Jayanagar Shopping Complex

The only route to the upper retail level is this staircase — no ramp, no lift. A wheelchair user or elderly shopper with a walker cannot reach an entire tier of this market's economy.

12.92881, 77.58452  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:30 PM

✕ BARRIER

Accessibility · Core Evidence

Medical Store, No Ramp

Jayanagar Medical Centre, 8th Main Rd

A pharmacy reached only via a raised step-only threshold. The irony is precise: a shop meant to serve people's health is itself closed to the elderly and mobility-impaired who need it most.

12.92885, 77.58348  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:40 PM

✕ BARRIER

Safety · Core Evidence

Unmarked Crossing

TTMC Plaza, 4th T Block East

Right outside the busiest transit hub in the 4th Block, a woman carrying a child crosses an open plaza with no marked crossing, signal, or refuge island.

12.92869, 77.58499  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:25 PM

✕ BARRIER

Accessibility · Core Evidence

Broken Footpath by Civic Office

10th Main Rd, near Laxmi Silks

Metres from the BBMP ward marshal's own post, the footpath is loose granite and litter. Visible civic presence has not translated into functional upkeep.

12.92859, 77.58509  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:23 PM

✕ BARRIER

Accessibility · Core Evidence

Footpath as Parking Lot

Jayanagar Shopping Complex

The pedestrian lane through the market is almost entirely occupied by parked two-wheelers. Elderly shoppers are visible threading through the narrow gap that remains.

12.92882, 77.58452  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:30 PM

✕ BARRIER

Safety · Core Evidence

Debris Blocks a Good Footpath

36th Cross Rd, 4th Block

A wide, tree-shaded footpath — steps from the well-planned avenue shown earlier — blocked mid-path by dumped debris, forcing pedestrians into the traffic lane.

12.92346, 77.58331  ·  27 Jun 2026, 02:11 PM

✕ BARRIER

Environmental Quality

Garbage at the Market Gate

Jayanagar Shopping Complex Gate

A decorative wrought-iron gate frames an uncleared garbage pile. Money was spent on the ironwork; none on a waste-collection schedule for the ground beneath it.

12.92878, 77.58449  ·  27 Jun 2026, 12:32 PM

Development Audit Scorecard

Scored 1 (very poor) to 5 (excellent)

Watch what happens when a dimension measures whether something exists versus whether everyone can use it.

Public Amenities4/5

Dense market, organised bus terminal, cafes and pharmacies within short walking distance.

Opportunities4/5

Market and Shopping Complex support diverse livelihoods, but not equally reachable by all.

Mobility & Transport3/5

TTMC terminal is organised and sheltered, but feeder footpaths are broken or parked over.

Accessibility2/5

No ramps, tactile paving or audible signals anywhere surveyed. Core finding of the audit.

Environmental Quality2/5

Uncleared garbage recurs at high-footfall points despite decent tree cover on some stretches.

Safety2/5

Debris forcing pedestrians into traffic and an unmarked crossing outside the main transit hub.

Inclusion2/5

Staircase-only market access and a medical store with no ramp exclude the elderly and disabled.

Every pin below is a real site from this audit, plotted where it happened. Click a pin for the finding.

Access — development working as intended Barrier — restricts who can use it

Sen's Capability Approach

Capability map

Development judged not by what exists, but by the real freedom people have to use it. Each capability Jayanagar genuinely expands is paired with where that same capability is restricted for a defined group of residents.

Capabilities expanded
Capabilities restricted

Bodily movement & recreation

The 35th Cross Rd park gives residents a flat, continuous, shaded path to walk and exercise.

Bodily movement in commerce

That freedom disappears at the Shopping Complex, where the only route upstairs is a staircase.

Health-seeking

A chemist and general store exists within easy reach of most residential lanes.

Health-seeking for the mobility-impaired

That same store is reachable only via a raised step-only threshold.

Safe public space

An organised, sheltered bus terminal offers a controlled waiting environment.

Safe movement around it

The plaza just outside has no marked crossing, forcing informal negotiation with traffic.

Environmental wellbeing

Wide, tree-shaded avenues show real investment in green, walkable streetscape.

Environmental wellbeing under waste

A structurally similar footpath metres away is blocked by dumped debris.

Civic trust

A visible BBMP ward office signals institutional presence in the neighbourhood.

Civic trust tested

The footpath immediately outside that same office is broken and littered.

A common-pool problem, in miniature. A footpath, like a park bench or a market gate, belongs to everyone — which is precisely why it tends to get appropriated by whoever asserts the space first: a scooter parked outside a shop, a stall spilling onto a walking lane. Individually rational, collectively a footpath that stops functioning as one — the tragedy of the commons, playing out on a pavement rather than a pasture.

Whose city is it? A staircase or a raised threshold is not a neutral fact — it is experienced differently depending on the body that meets it. A locality's real inclusiveness is tested not by its flagship spaces, but by whether its most ordinary ones — a market entrance, a chemist's doorstep — remain usable by its least mobile residents.

Capability Enhancement Proposal

Recommended intervention

RECOMMENDED

Problem

Despite being one of Bengaluru's most developed and highest-valued neighbourhoods, Jayanagar 4th Block remains only partially accessible for senior citizens, persons with disabilities, and ordinary pedestrians — evidenced architecturally by a staircase-only shopping complex and a rampless medical store, and structurally by broken footpaths, encroachment, and an unmarked crossing outside its main transit hub.

Proposed intervention

  • Universal accessibility audit of all public roads, market lanes, and essential-service premises (clinics, pharmacies) in the 4th Block.
  • Barrier-free footpaths and ramps, prioritising step-free access into the Shopping Complex and single-step storefronts like Jayanagar Medical Centre.
  • Tactile paving for the visually impaired along key desire lines — market entrance to bus terminal.
  • Marked pedestrian crossings with audible signals at the TTMC plaza and other unmarked high-footfall points.
  • Regular maintenance funded through ward-level budgets, paired with designated parking bays set back from footpaths.

Development link

Accessibility is not a cosmetic add-on to development but a precondition for the freedoms — to move, work, seek healthcare, and participate in public life — that Sen's Capability Approach argues development should ultimately be judged by. A locality can score well on every conventional growth indicator and still fail this test for a defined group of its own residents.

Schematic comparison: current condition (footpath consumed by parking/debris, no ramp) vs. proposed universal-access redesign (obstruction-free footpath, tactile guide strip, kerb ramp, marked parking, audible signal).

FIELD AUDIT — JAYANAGAR 4TH BLOCK, BENGALURU — PREPARED 27 JUNE 2026 — CASE FILE JN-4B/2026
